metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an in-repo grounding for the `schema=` parameter: either a pinned experiment log entry showing `inspect.signature(repair_json)` includes `schema` for json-repair>=0.30, or a unit test that calls `repair_json('{}', schema={}, return_objects=True)` and asserts no TypeError, so the AC's library-behavior claim is empirically backed.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The pinned json-repair exposes schema= + schema_repair_mode (verified: repair_json params include schema), so the consumer interface is present; AC confirms the installed version accepts it' — but there is no in-repo experiment log, import probe, unit test, or changelog reference grounding that the installed json-repair>=0.30 version actually supports the schema= and schema_repair_mode parameters. The string 'schema_repair_mode' appears nowhere in the repo. If this parameter was introduced in a later release than >=0.30 covers, or was introduced under a different name, AC item 3 ('json_repair is called with schema=<model>') will silently fail at runtime.
findings.location: What section — Output self-heal bullet (1): 'The pinned json-repair exposes schema= + schema_repair_mode (verified: repair_json params include schema)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the truncation conjunction to the NativeOutput branch: after `run_result = agent.run_sync(...)` in the NativeOutput path, add a `check_stop_reason` call on `run_result`'s finish_reason before returning, so schema-valid-but-truncated verdicts are caught on all providers.
findings.finding: The plan's truncation conjunction (schema-valid AND untruncated = pass condition) is only applied in the PromptedOutput retry loop. The NativeOutput path (`runner.py:497-502`) returns `run_result.output` directly after `agent.run_sync()` with no `check_stop_reason` call, so a `finish_reason=length` truncation on OpenAI/Google/Groq native structured output is never detected — the truncated-but-schema-valid verdict silently returns as PASS/BLOCK.
findings.location: What section, 'Truncation-within-a-schema-valid-verdict' bullet; runner.py:497-502 (_pai_structured NativeOutput branch)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `json-repair>=0.30` actually exposes `repair_json(schema=…)` and `schema_repair_mode` by citing the library changelog, its API docs, or a minimal offline test snippet — and update the AC / scope if the parameter is absent or behaves differently than described.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `json-repair` exposes a `schema=` parameter and `schema_repair_mode` ('verified: `repair_json` params include `schema`'), but no experiment log, doc quote, or code citation in the repo backs this claim. The existing research doc describes json-repair solely as a syntactic fixer (fences/commas/quotes); `schema_repair_mode` and `schema=` are not mentioned anywhere. The pinned version (`>=0.30`) is consistent with the basic fixer, not a schema-aware variant. If these parameters don't exist or behave differently, AC 3 ('`json_repair` is called with `schema=<model>`') cannot be satisfied.
findings.location: What section — output self-heal delta (1); AC item 3; `src/rebar/llm/structured.py:161`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line offline experiment to the story's experiment log: `from json_repair import repair_json; import inspect; print(inspect.signature(repair_json))`. If `schema=` is absent, replace this delta with post-repair Pydantic coercion (which the existing `validate_to` already provides) and update AC 3 accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an experiment-log entry that confirms the exact pydantic-ai `ModelResponse.provider_details` sub-field path for an OpenAI content-filter response (e.g. `.incomplete_details.reason` vs another attribute), citing the pydantic-ai source or a canned offline test analogous to the `finish_reason` confirmation in `test_llm_failure_matrix.py`.
findings.finding: The refusal-as-content detection rule keys on the nested path `provider_details`/`incomplete_details.reason == 'content_filter'` (OpenAI Responses API). The plan's experiment log verifies only that `provider_details` is present as a top-level `ModelResponse` field — it does NOT verify the sub-structure `.incomplete_details.reason`. Pydantic-ai's `provider_details` is an opaque provider-specific blob; the specific nested attribute path for OpenAI's content-filter signal has not been confirmed in any experiment, doc reference, or code citation in the repo.
findings.location: What section — refusal-as-content protocol; Verified library behavior section (experiment log)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the `json-repair` pin in `pyproject.toml` to the minimum version that introduces the `schema=` parameter, confirmed by the experiment log's `json_repair.__version__`, so the AC is guaranteed by the dependency constraint.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `json-repair`'s `schema=` parameter is available ('verified: `repair_json` params include `schema`'), but `pyproject.toml` pins `json-repair>=0.30` with no upper bound and no minimum version that guarantees the `schema=` API. The `schema=` parameter was introduced in a much later release than 0.30. If the dependency resolves to the lower bound, `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True, schema=<model>)` raises `TypeError`, silently breaking the entire repair path.
findings.location: What section — Output self-heal bullet (1) / pyproject.toml:111 / AC item 3
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, change `"json-repair>=0.30"` to `"json-repair>=<version-that-introduces-schema>"` where `<version>` is determined from the experiment log's `import json_repair; json_repair.__version__` output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the `json-repair` pin in `pyproject.toml` to the minimum version that introduces the `schema=` parameter (confirm via `json_repair.__version__` in the experiment log) and add it as a dependency constraint so `schema=<model>` is guaranteed available at the lower bound.
findings.finding: The plan claims `repair_json` accepts a `schema=` argument ("verified: `repair_json` params include `schema`") but the pinned lower-bound in pyproject.toml is `json-repair>=0.30` with no upper bound. The `schema=` / `schema_repair_mode` parameters were introduced in a much later version of json-repair than 0.30. If the installed version resolves to the lower bound, the call `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True, schema=<model>)` raises `TypeError` (unexpected keyword argument), silently breaking the repair path or causing test failures.
findings.location: What section, structured.py:161 / pyproject.toml line 111
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, replace `"json-repair>=0.30"` with `"json-repair>={MIN_VERSION_WITH_SCHEMA_PARAM}"` where MIN_VERSION_WITH_SCHEMA_PARAM is confirmed from the json-repair changelog. Also add a guard in structured.py (inspect `repair_json` signature or catch `TypeError`) to fail clearly if the version constraint is violated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document the minimum `json-repair` version that introduced the `schema=` / `schema_repair_mode` parameters (check `json_repair.__version__` or the library changelog), then tighten `pyproject.toml`'s pin to at least that version (e.g. `json-repair>=X.Y`) so the `repair_json(schema=<model>)` call in AC-3 cannot silently fail at the lower bound.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `repair_json` accepts `schema=<model>` and `schema_repair_mode` parameters ('verified: repair_json params include schema'), but the dependency pin `json-repair>=0.30` in pyproject.toml sets only a lower bound without naming the minimum version that introduced `schema=`. If the installed version resolves at or near 0.30 (which predates the schema= feature), the call `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True, schema=<model>)` will raise `TypeError: unexpected keyword argument`, silently breaking the AC-3 repair path or crashing the test suite.
findings.location: What section ('The pinned json-repair exposes schema= + schema_repair_mode (verified: repair_json params include schema)') / pyproject.toml:111 / src/rebar/llm/structured.py:161
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, replace `"json-repair>=0.30"` with a pin that sets a floor at the version introducing `schema=` (confirm via the json-repair changelog at https://github.com/mangiucugna/json_repair/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md). Also add a one-line smoke test in the existing test_structured.py that calls `repair_json('', return_objects=True, schema={})` to catch a TypeError at import time if the version regresses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the exact `provider_details` sub-path for OpenAI content-filter refusal-as-content by adding an offline FunctionModel injection test (analogous to `test_llm_failure_matrix.py`'s `_canned()` pattern) that constructs a `ModelResponse` with `provider_details` carrying a simulated `incomplete_details.reason='content_filter'` and confirms the detection fires — or document the exact pydantic-ai 1.107.0 field path in the experiment log.
findings.finding: The refusal-as-content detection keys on `provider_details`/`incomplete_details.reason == 'content_filter'` as the structured OpenAI out-of-band signal, but the plan's experiment log only confirms `provider_details present` as a top-level field — it does not confirm the internal sub-path `incomplete_details.reason` actually exists on the pydantic-ai 1.107.0 `ModelResponse.provider_details` object for OpenAI content-filter responses. If the field path is wrong (e.g. the actual key is `output_filters` or the structure differs by provider), the detection silently no-ops rather than raising an error.
findings.location: What section ('flag refusal-as-content when the response carries non-empty text parts AND provider_details/incomplete_details.reason == "content_filter"')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either extend the plan to apply the finish_reason + provider_details checks to the NativeOutput path (runner.py:497-502) as well, or explicitly scope the runtime result-validation to the PromptedOutput path only and explain why the NativeOutput path is exempt (e.g., provider-enforced constrained decoding makes certain failures structurally impossible there).
findings.finding: The plan claims 'Runtime result-validation on every gate call' but the NativeOutput branch (runner.py:497-502) returns run_result.output directly — no finish_reason read and no provider_details check — leaving that path uncovered by the proposed refusal-as-content and truncation-conjunction checks.
findings.location: What section: 'Runtime result-validation on every gate call: reuse the existing check_stop_reason'; runner.py lines 497-502 (NativeOutput branch)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact call-chain from LLMOutcome construction in runner.py/_pai_structured to the verdict's coverage dict (e.g., whether _pai_structured raises a new exception carrying the LLMOutcome, or returns it in a wrapper, or whether gate_dispatch.py/orchestrator.py are added to the Files list as edit targets for the coverage-write seam).
findings.finding: The plan says detected silent-successes are 'surfaced via coverage' via the shared LLMOutcome, but _pai_structured returns (parsed_obj, usage_dict) and has no mechanism to write to a coverage dict. The callers that would receive and route the LLMOutcome to coverage (gate_dispatch.py, orchestrator.py, plan_review/__init__.py etc.) are not enumerated as edit targets.

Similarly, the plan says 'runner.py:490's reask' but line 490 is inside the docstring of _pai_structured; the reask prompt construction is at lines 529-533.

findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'structured.py:154 currently calls repair_json(cand, return_objects=True)' to 'structured.py:161 currently calls repair_json(cand, return_objects=True)'; change 'runner.py:490's reask' to 'runner.py:529's reask prompt construction'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan's line-number references: change `structured.py:154` to `structured.py:161` (the actual `repair_json` call) and `runner.py:490` to `runner.py:529-533` (the actual reask prompt construction inside `_pai_structured`).
findings.finding: Plan states 'structured.py:154' for the repair_json call and 'runner.py:490' for the reask prompt, but both line numbers are stale: the actual repair_json call is at structured.py line 161, and the reask prompt construction is at runner.py lines 529-533 (inside _pai_structured which starts at 480).

findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'structured.py:154 currently calls repair_json(cand, return_objects=True)' with 'structured.py:161 currently calls repair_json(cand, return_objects=True)'; replace 'runner.py:490's reask' with 'runner.py:529-533's reask'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit AC (and corresponding test cases) for `provider_details`-absent, `incomplete_details`-missing, and unrecognized-reason-string inputs to the refusal-as-content detector, specifying the expected fallback behavior (e.g. treat as non-refusal, log a warning).
findings.finding: The refusal-as-content detection introduces new conditional logic that keys on `provider_details`/`incomplete_details.reason`, but neither the AC nor the testing notes specify behavior when `provider_details` is absent, `None`, or contains an unexpected/unrecognized reason string. This is a new user-facing detection flow whose invalid/boundary paths are unspecified.
findings.location: AC line 1 ('Refusal-as-content … are detected and routed') and 'What' section — refusal-as-content protocol paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: 'When provider_details is absent or incomplete_details.reason is unrecognized, the detector defaults to non-refusal (does not route) and emits a structured warning; explicit test cases cover provider_details=None and reason="unknown_future_value".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit fallback rule for the refusal-as-content detector: specify what happens when `provider_details` is absent, `incomplete_details` is missing, or the reason is unrecognized (e.g. 'treat as non-refusal, do not route as CHANGE_INPUT — only the confirmed content_filter signal triggers routing').
findings.finding: The refusal-as-content detection rule specifies `provider_details`/`incomplete_details.reason == "content_filter"` as the detection signal but provides no fallback for the cases where `provider_details` is absent, `incomplete_details` is missing from it, or the reason string is an unrecognized value. This is a T8(d) gap: the detection sub-step has no specified fallback for the incomplete/failed case.
findings.location: What section — Refusal-as-content protocol paragraph: 'flag refusal-as-content when the response carries non-empty text parts AND `provider_details`/`incomplete_details.reason == "content_filter"'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the refusal-as-content protocol: 'If provider_details is None or incomplete_details.reason is absent or unrecognized, the response is treated as non-refusal (fall through to the normal verdict path); only an explicit content_filter signal triggers CHANGE_INPUT routing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete attribute access path from `AgentRunResult` to `provider_details`/`incomplete_details.reason` for both OpenAI Responses and Anthropic (e.g. `getattr(getattr(result.response, 'provider_details', None), 'incomplete_details', None)`) so the implementer uses the correct path and handles None safely.
findings.finding: The refusal-as-content detection keys on `provider_details`/`incomplete_details.reason` from the `ModelResponse`, but the plan does not specify the attribute access path from the `AgentRunResult` object (`result`) available inside the `_pai_structured` retry loop. The experiment log confirms `provider_details` is a field on `ModelResponse`, but `result` is an `AgentRunResult` — the path (e.g. `result.response`, `result.all_messages()[-1]`, or another accessor) is unspecified, leaving the implementer to guess and potentially access the wrong object or silently get None.
findings.location: What section, 'Refusal-as-content protocol' bullet; Verified library behavior section

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit observability requirement: each new routing path (refusal-as-content detected, truncation-conjunction triggered) must emit at least one structured log line or metric increment so operators can observe and alert on these events.
findings.finding: The new detection/routing paths (refusal-as-content fires, truncation-conjunction fires) have no explicit observability requirement — no log line, metric increment, or trace event is specified — so operators cannot distinguish 'review returned no verdict because the model was silently refused' from normal flow in production.
findings.location: ## What — Refusal-as-content protocol; Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `schema_repair_mode` value to use (e.g., `schema_repair_mode='lenient'`) so the structured.py:154 change is fully specified without executor judgment.
findings.finding: The `schema_repair_mode` argument value to pass alongside `schema=<model>` in the `json-repair` call is not specified — the plan confirms the parameter exists but does not state which mode to select, leaving a real design choice to the executor.
findings.location: What section / structured.py:154 delta / AC: '`json_repair` is called with `schema=<model>`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `schema_repair_mode` value to use in the `repair_json(schema=<model>)` call (e.g. `schema_repair_mode='best_effort'`) so the call site is fully defined and the AC is mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan confirms `json-repair` exposes a `schema_repair_mode` parameter alongside `schema=<model>` but never specifies which mode value to pass. This is a decision rule referenced without a defined value vocabulary: an executor calling `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True, schema=<model>)` must independently choose the mode (e.g. 'best_effort' vs 'strict' vs another library-defined value), and the AC's 'AC confirms the installed version accepts it' does not name the mode, making the call site underspecified.
findings.location: What section — Output self-heal bullet (1): 'The pinned `json-repair` exposes `schema=` + `schema_repair_mode` (verified: `repair_json` params include `schema`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Output self-heal' bullet, change to: 'call `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True, schema=<model>, schema_repair_mode="best_effort")` — using best_effort mode so partial repairs that already pass tolerant_parse are not regressed by strict schema enforcement.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the schema-guided repair call is wrapped in a try/except with a fallback to schema-less repair_json, so a library version change cannot bypass the self-heal ladder.
findings.finding: The plan specifies calling `repair_json(cand, schema=<model>)` and asserts the installed version accepts it, but specifies no fallback if the schema-arg call itself raises (e.g., after a dependency update or version pin relaxation) — a failure in the repair call could propagate as an unhandled exception, bypassing the existing self-heal ladder.
findings.location: ## What — Output self-heal, structured.py:154 delta; Acceptance Criteria line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap as: `try: repaired = repair_json(cand, schema=model, return_objects=True) except TypeError: repaired = repair_json(cand, return_objects=True)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `runner.py:490` reask-message-format change section, enumerate (or explicitly disclaim the absence of) existing tests/snapshots that assert on the current reask message content, so the implementer knows what to update.
findings.finding: The plan changes the reask message format in runner.py:490 (adds bounded faulty prior output to the existing instructions+schema_hint+error string) but does not acknowledge existing tests or callers that may assert on the old reask message content, risking undetected test breakage.
findings.location: runner.py:490 / What section (output self-heal delta 2) / AC: 'json_repair is called with schema=<model>; the targeted reask includes the bounded faulty prior output'

findings.finding: The truncation limit for the 'bounded faulty prior output' snippet added to the reask prompt is not specified — the plan says '`str(result.output)`, truncated' but leaves the truncation threshold undefined, making the AC ('targeted reask includes the bounded faulty prior output') non-executable without an implicit choice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency edge on story 30ff (or specify a local stub/interface contract) so that the routing AC can be verified end-to-end in CI regardless of merge order.
findings.finding: The routing AC ('detected and routed through story 30ff's shared LLMOutcome') has no explicit dependency edge on story 30ff being merged (or a stub/mock interface being available), so the proving command `make test` may pass only because the routing call is untestable and silently skipped.
findings.location: What section / AC: 'Refusal-as-content... are detected and routed, not returned as a PASS/BLOCK verdict' / handoff note 'Handoff interface: 30ff exposes the LLMOutcome constructor + sanitizer that 978d calls'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Depends-on: story 30ff (LLMOutcome constructor + sanitizer)' line to the plan, or specify: 'If 30ff is not merged, use a local stub `LLMOutcome` with the same constructor signature so the routing test exercises the full call path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the two story-ID aliases for the shared-LLMOutcome dependency: replace either '30ff' or 'mamba' (whichever is wrong) with the canonical ID everywhere in the plan so the handoff interface is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan refers to the shared-LLMOutcome dependency story by two different names in two different sections: 'story 30ff' (What section, refusal-as-content protocol) and 'story mamba' (Verified library behavior section, final sentence). An implementer coordinating the handoff interface cannot determine which story ID is authoritative.
findings.location: What section (refusal-as-content protocol paragraph) vs. Verified library behavior section (final sentence)
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one canonical ID (likely the one used in the project tracker) and do a global replace in the plan; add a parenthetical alias if both names are in use (e.g., 'story 30ff (a.k.a. mamba)').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out statement (e.g., 'revert to schema-less repair_json call' and 'revert to original reask message') or a paired regression test that pins current repair success rate, so the behavior change is a justified, reversible choice rather than an incidental edit.
findings.finding: The two behavior-changing edits to existing code — adding `schema=<model>` to `repair_json` in structured.py:154 and changing the reask message in runner.py:490 — alter currently-working repair/retry paths but no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequencing is described. Adding `schema=` to json-repair could make the repair step MORE restrictive, causing previously-repaired payloads to fail differently; the plan only verifies the library accepts the parameter, not the regression risk.
findings.location: What section (Output self-heal deltas: structured.py:154 and runner.py:490); Acceptance Criteria AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the refusal-as-content detection rule (provider fields keyed on, why regex is rejected), the truncation-conjunction pass condition, and the shared-LLMOutcome routing contract.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel multi-component detection protocol (refusal-as-content via provider_details, truncation-conjunction, routing through shared LLMOutcome) but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture the architectural decision — future maintainers have no durable record of why these detection rules exist, which provider fields they key on, or what the routing contract is.
findings.location: ## What (Refusal-as-content protocol) and ## Scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the faulty-prior-output truncation bound as a named, configurable constant (e.g., MAX_FAULTY_SNIPPET_CHARS) rather than an inline magic truncation, and document the chosen default value in the AC.
findings.finding: The 'bounded snippet of the model's faulty prior reply' added to the reask in runner.py:490 is described as 'str(result.output), truncated' but no maximum length, configurable constant, or truncation strategy is specified — the truncation bound is hardcoded-by-omission and will likely evolve as models and token budgets change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `schema=` must receive the JSON Schema dict (`model_cls.model_json_schema()`), not the Pydantic class itself — update the plan text and AC to read `schema=model_cls.model_json_schema()` so the implementer passes the correct type.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'add `schema=<model>`' to the `repair_json` call, but `json_repair`'s `schema=` parameter expects a JSON Schema dict (e.g. the output of `model_cls.model_json_schema()`), not a Pydantic model class object. Passing the class directly would either fail at runtime or be silently ignored, defeating the schema-guided repair entirely.
findings.location: What section, Output self-heal bullet (1); AC: '`json_repair` is called with `schema=<model>`'; structured.py:161

The plan names `structured.py` and `runner.py` as Files but the verdict-finalization path has multiple candidates: `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`, `gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict`, `workflow_ops`, or the `findings.validate_structured` seam. This is a T8(c) ambiguous-placement gap for a new state check.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
